facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from diverticul/o (small pouch/sac) + -itis (inflammation), meaning inflammation of pouches that form in the intestinal wall?
facts.explanation: Diverticulitis breaks down as: diverticul/o (small pouch or sac, from Latin 'diverticulum' — a byway or side road) + -itis (inflammation). Diverticula are small pouches that protrude through the muscular wall of the colon (most commonly the sigmoid colon). When one becomes blocked with fecal matter and inflamed, the condition is diverticulitis. Symptoms include left lower quadrant pain, fever, and change in bowel habits. A low-fiber diet is a major risk factor.
facts.statement: Diverticulitis means inflammation of diverticula (pouches in the intestinal wall), from diverticul/o (small pouch) + -itis (inflammation).
facts.wowFactor: Diverticular disease was virtually unknown before 1900. Its dramatic rise in Western countries throughout the 20th century correlates exactly with the introduction of refined flour and reduced dietary fiber — making it a disease of modern processed eating.

diverticul/o (small pouch) + -itis (inflammation) = ___

facts.quizQuestion: What condition derives from hem/o (blood) + rhoid (flowing/stream), describing swollen vascular cushions in and around the rectum?
facts.explanation: Hemorrhoids derives from Greek 'haimorrhois' — haima (blood) + rhein (to flow) — literally 'blood flowing'. The term reflects the bleeding that swollen rectal veins produce. Hemorrhoids are actually normal vascular cushions present in everyone; the condition arises when they become swollen, inflamed, or prolapsed. They are classified as internal (above the dentate line, usually painless) or external (below, often painful). Straining at stool, constipation, and pregnancy are major contributing factors.
facts.statement: Hemorrhoids derives from Greek hem/o (blood) + rhoid (flowing), describing swollen venous cushions in the rectum and anus.
facts.wowFactor: Napoleon Bonaparte famously suffered from severe hemorrhoids at the Battle of Waterloo in 1815. Some historians argue his hemorrhoidal pain prevented him from mounting his horse to personally direct the battle, contributing to his defeat and exile.

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from hydr/o (water) + cephal/o (head) + -us (noun), meaning abnormal accumulation of cerebrospinal fluid in the brain?
facts.explanation: Hydrocephalus breaks down as: hydr/o (water, from Greek 'hydor') + cephal/o (head, from Greek 'kephale') + -us (noun suffix). It means 'water on the brain'. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) normally circulates through the ventricles and around the brain; when its flow, absorption, or production is disrupted, it accumulates and expands the ventricles, increasing intracranial pressure. In infants (before skull fusion), the head visibly enlarges. Treatment is surgical, using a shunt to drain excess CSF.
facts.statement: Hydrocephalus means water in the head, from hydr/o (water) + cephal/o (head), describing abnormal CSF accumulation in the brain ventricles.
facts.wowFactor: The Ommaya reservoir — a device implanted under the scalp to access CSF — was invented in 1963. Today, programmable ventricular shunts can be adjusted non-invasively using a magnet held to the patient's head, changing CSF drainage rates without surgery.

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from cerebr/o (brain) + vascul/o (vessel) + -ar (pertaining to) + accident, meaning a sudden interruption of blood supply to the brain?
facts.explanation: Cerebrovascular accident (CVA) breaks down as: cerebr/o (brain, from Latin 'cerebrum') + vascul/o (vessel, from Latin 'vasculum') + -ar (pertaining to) + accident (sudden event). A CVA, commonly called a stroke, occurs when brain tissue is deprived of blood — either by blockage (ischemic stroke, ~85% of cases) or by bleeding (hemorrhagic stroke, ~15%). The FAST acronym identifies symptoms: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency services.
facts.statement: Cerebrovascular accident means a sudden vascular event in the brain, from cerebr/o (brain) + vascul/o (vessel), commonly called a stroke.
facts.wowFactor: Every minute a stroke goes untreated, approximately 1.9 million neurons die. This led to the phrase 'time is brain' in emergency medicine — driving the shift from 'stroke' to 'brain attack' to create the same urgency as 'heart attack'.

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from para- (abnormal/beside) + esthesi/o (sensation) + -ia (condition), meaning abnormal sensations such as tingling, numbness, or 'pins and needles'?
facts.explanation: Paresthesia breaks down as: para- (beside, abnormal, from Greek 'para') + esthesi/o (sensation/feeling, from Greek 'aisthesis') + -ia (condition). It describes abnormal spontaneous sensations including tingling, prickling, burning, or numbness — the 'pins and needles' feeling. Common causes include compression of a nerve (sitting on your foot), vitamin B12 deficiency, diabetes, multiple sclerosis, and Raynaud's phenomenon. Compare anesthesia (an- = without sensation) and hyperesthesia (hyper- = excessive sensation).
facts.statement: Paresthesia means abnormal sensation (tingling, numbness), from para- (abnormal) + esthesi/o (sensation) + -ia (condition).
facts.wowFactor: Paresthesia from crossing your legs cuts off blood to the peroneal nerve, causing the familiar 'pins and needles'. The sensation when blood flow returns is actually the nerve misfiring as it reperfuses — your nervous system literally rebooting.

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from oste/o (bone) + arthr/o (joint) + -itis (inflammation), describing degenerative cartilage breakdown in joints?
facts.explanation: Osteoarthritis breaks down as: oste/o (bone, from Greek 'osteon') + arthr/o (joint, from Greek 'arthron') + -itis (inflammation). Despite the -itis suffix, OA is primarily a degenerative process rather than inflammatory — it involves breakdown and loss of articular cartilage, subchondral bone thickening, and osteophyte (bone spur) formation. It is the most common form of arthritis worldwide, affecting weight-bearing joints (knees, hips, spine) and the hands. Risk factors include age, obesity, joint injury, and genetics.
facts.statement: Osteoarthritis means bone-joint inflammation (degenerative), from oste/o (bone) + arthr/o (joint) + -itis, describing cartilage breakdown in joints.
facts.wowFactor: Osteoarthritis has been found in dinosaur fossils — a hadrosaur (duck-billed dinosaur) from 70 million years ago shows classic OA changes in its spine. Joint degeneration is apparently an ancient vertebrate problem, not a modern one.

facts.quizQuestion: What autoimmune condition is named from rheum/o (flowing/watery) + -oid (resembling) + arthr/o (joint) + -itis (inflammation), describing chronic inflammatory joint destruction?
facts.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis breaks down as: rheum/o (flowing, watery discharge, from Greek 'rheuma') + -oid (resembling) + arthr/o (joint) + -itis (inflammation). The 'rheuma' referred to the ancient Greek theory of flowing humors causing joint pain. RA is a systemic autoimmune disease in which the immune system attacks the synovial lining of joints, causing symmetric joint swelling, pain, stiffness (especially morning stiffness), and eventual joint destruction. The small joints of the hands and feet are typically affected first.
facts.statement: Rheumatoid arthritis describes flowing/watery joint inflammation (autoimmune), from rheum/o (flowing) + -oid (resembling) + arthr/o (joint) + -itis (inflammation).
facts.wowFactor: Rheumatoid arthritis is 2-3 times more common in women than men. The discovery of methotrexate as an RA treatment was accidental — it was developed as a chemotherapy drug, and physicians noticed it also dramatically reduced joint inflammation.

variants.correctAnswer: Flowing watery humors thought to cause pain

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from spondyl/o (vertebra) + -osis (degenerative condition), meaning degenerative changes and wear-and-tear of the spinal vertebrae?
facts.explanation: Spondylosis breaks down as: spondyl/o (vertebra, from Greek 'spondylos') + -osis (degenerative or abnormal condition). It describes age-related degenerative changes of the intervertebral discs, vertebral bodies, and facet joints, often producing osteophytes (bone spurs). When it affects the cervical spine it is cervical spondylosis; the lumbar spine, lumbar spondylosis. It is extremely common — visible on imaging in over 90% of adults over 65. Contrast with spondylitis (-itis = inflammation, as in ankylosing spondylitis).
facts.statement: Spondylosis means vertebral degeneration, from spondyl/o (vertebra) + -osis (degenerative condition).
facts.wowFactor: Spondylosis has been found in Egyptian mummies — ancient Egyptians who survived into middle age almost universally developed spinal degeneration, just like modern populations. Even Ramesses II (died ~1213 BCE) showed extensive cervical spondylosis on CT scanning.

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from rhabd/o (rod-shaped) + my/o (muscle) + -lysis (breakdown/dissolution), meaning the breakdown of skeletal muscle fibers releasing myoglobin into the bloodstream?
facts.explanation: Rhabdomyolysis breaks down as: rhabd/o (rod-shaped, from Greek 'rhabdos' — striated/rod) + my/o (muscle) + -lysis (breakdown, dissolution). The rhabdo- refers to the rod-like striations of skeletal muscle (as opposed to smooth muscle). In rhabdomyolysis, skeletal muscle fibers break down and release their contents — especially myoglobin — into the bloodstream. Myoglobin is toxic to the kidneys, making acute kidney injury the primary complication. Classic presentation includes muscle pain, weakness, and dark 'cola-colored' urine (myoglobinuria).
facts.statement: Rhabdomyolysis means rod-muscle breakdown, from rhabd/o (rod-shaped/striated) + my/o (muscle) + -lysis (breakdown), describing skeletal muscle fiber destruction.
facts.wowFactor: Crush syndrome from earthquake victims — first described after the 1908 Messina earthquake and systematically studied after the 1940 London Blitz — is rhabdomyolysis caused by prolonged muscle compression. Survivors of being trapped for hours paradoxically develop kidney failure after rescue when the crushed muscle releases its contents.

rhabd/o (rod) + my/o (muscle) + -lysis (breakdown) = ___

facts.quizQuestion: What skin condition is named from the Latin 'urtica' (stinging nettle), characterized by raised, itchy wheals appearing suddenly on the skin?
facts.explanation: Urticaria comes from Latin 'urtica' meaning stinging nettle — because the itchy, burning wheals (hives) resemble the skin reaction from touching a nettle plant. The wheals are caused by mast cell degranulation releasing histamine, which causes localized edema in the dermis. Triggers include allergens, medications (especially NSAIDs, antibiotics), foods, infections, cold, pressure, or emotional stress. In acute urticaria, lesions resolve within 24 hours; chronic urticaria persists beyond 6 weeks.
facts.statement: Urticaria (hives) is named from the Latin 'urtica' (stinging nettle), describing raised, itchy wheals caused by histamine release in the skin.
facts.wowFactor: Dermographism — 'skin writing' — is a form of urticaria where firm stroking of the skin causes wheals to appear in the stroke pattern within minutes. About 5% of the population can literally write on their own skin with their fingernail and have the raised letters remain readable for 30 minutes.

facts.quizQuestion: What chronic skin condition is named from the Greek 'psora' (itch), characterized by thickened, scaly plaques caused by accelerated skin cell turnover?
facts.explanation: Psoriasis comes from Greek 'psora' meaning itch or mange (from 'psoriasis' — being itchy). It is a chronic autoimmune condition in which T-cell activation accelerates keratinocyte (skin cell) proliferation from the normal 28-day cycle to just 3-4 days. This creates thickened, silvery-scaled plaques most commonly on elbows, knees, scalp, and lower back. The Auspitz sign — pinpoint bleeding when a scale is lifted — is characteristic. Psoriasis is associated with psoriatic arthritis in up to 30% of patients.
facts.statement: Psoriasis means the itching condition, from Greek 'psora' (itch), describing the scaly, itchy plaques of this autoimmune skin disease.
facts.wowFactor: Psoriasis has affected famous people throughout history — King Philip II of Spain had severe psoriasis, and there is debate that Goliath (the biblical giant) may have had psoriasis based on ancient descriptions. About 2-3% of the world population is affected.

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from lymph/o (lymph/lymphatic system) + -oma (tumor), meaning cancer originating in the lymphatic system's lymphocytes?
facts.explanation: Lymphoma breaks down as: lymph/o (lymph, lymphatic tissue, from Latin 'lympha' — clear water) + -oma (tumor, from Greek 'oma'). It refers to cancers arising from lymphocytes (white blood cells of the lymphatic system). The two main categories are Hodgkin lymphoma (characterized by Reed-Sternberg cells, generally very treatable) and non-Hodgkin lymphoma (a diverse group, over 60 subtypes). Swollen lymph nodes (lymphadenopathy), night sweats, unexplained fever, and weight loss ('B symptoms') are classic presentations.
facts.statement: Lymphoma means lymphatic tumor, from lymph/o (lymph) + -oma (tumor), describing cancer of the lymphatic system's lymphocytes.
facts.wowFactor: Hodgkin lymphoma was one of the first cancers to be curable with radiation therapy and chemotherapy — a landmark in oncology history. Thomas Hodgkin, who first described it in 1832, could not have imagined that by the late 20th century 80-90% of early-stage patients would be cured.

lymph/o (lymph tissue) + -oma (tumor) = ___

facts.quizQuestion: What condition derives from Greek 'aneurysma' (widening/dilation), describing an abnormal bulging or ballooning of a blood vessel wall?
facts.explanation: Aneurysm derives from Greek 'aneurysma' (a widening) — from 'ana' (up/throughout) + 'eurys' (wide). It describes a localized dilation (bulge) of a blood vessel wall, typically an artery, due to weakness in the vessel wall. Types include saccular (berry-shaped outpouching) and fusiform (spindle-shaped dilation). Aortic aneurysms and cerebral (berry) aneurysms are the most clinically significant. Rupture of a cerebral aneurysm causes subarachnoid hemorrhage — described by patients as 'the worst headache of my life'.
facts.statement: Aneurysm means widening or dilation (Greek 'aneurysma'), describing a bulging weakness in a blood vessel wall.
facts.wowFactor: The aortic aneurysm that killed Albert Einstein in 1955 had been identified years earlier. He was offered surgery but declined, reportedly saying 'It is tasteless to prolong life artificially. I have done my share; it is time to go.'

A ruptured cerebral (berry) aneurysm typically causes which type of bleed?

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from a- (without) + men/o (menstruation/month) + -rrhea (flow/discharge), meaning the absence of menstrual periods?
facts.explanation: Amenorrhea breaks down as: a- (without, absence of) + men/o (menstruation, month, from Greek 'men') + -rrhea (flow, discharge, from Greek 'rhein' — to flow). It means 'without menstrual flow'. Primary amenorrhea is when menstruation never begins by age 15-16. Secondary amenorrhea is when established periods stop for 3+ months (non-pregnant). Causes include pregnancy (most common), low body fat (athletes, anorexia), hyperprolactinemia, thyroid disorders, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), and premature ovarian insufficiency.
facts.statement: Amenorrhea means absence of menstrual flow, from a- (without) + men/o (menstruation) + -rrhea (flow).
facts.wowFactor: The 'female athlete triad' — amenorrhea + disordered eating + osteoporosis — occurs because extreme exercise and caloric restriction suppress reproductive hormones so severely that bones begin losing density. Elite female athletes can have the bone density of 50-year-old women.

variants.question: Break down 'amenorrhea' — what does men/o mean?
a- (without) + men/o (menstruation) + -rrhea (flow) = ___

facts.visualDescription: Calendar with menstruation cycle days marked absent, word AMENORRHEA split: A + MEN + RRHEA

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from presby- (old/elder) + -opia (vision condition), meaning age-related loss of near-vision as the eye lens loses flexibility?
facts.explanation: Presbyopia breaks down as: presby- (old man/elder, from Greek 'presbys') + -opia (vision, eye condition, from Greek 'ops'). It literally means 'old man's vision'. Starting around age 40-45, the crystalline lens of the eye progressively loses elasticity, reducing the ability to accommodate (focus on near objects). This is a universal age-related change — not a disease — and is the reason most people eventually need reading glasses. It is distinct from hyperopia (far-sightedness), which results from eyeball shape, not lens stiffness.
facts.statement: Presbyopia means old-person vision, from presby- (elder) + -opia (vision), describing age-related loss of near-focusing ability due to lens stiffening.
facts.wowFactor: Presbyopia affects nearly every human who lives past 45 — it is one of the few truly universal age-related changes. Benjamin Franklin, tired of swapping between his reading glasses and distance glasses, invented bifocal lenses around 1784 specifically to address presbyopia.

variants.question: Break down 'presbyopia' — what does presby- mean?
presby- (elder) + -opia (vision) = ___ (age-related near-vision loss)

facts.visualDescription: Eye diagram with stiff lens indicated by arrows, blurry close-up text in foreground, word PRESBYOPIA split: PRESBY + OPIA

facts.quizQuestion: What eye condition derives from Greek 'glaukos' (grey-blue/gleaming), in which elevated intraocular pressure damages the optic nerve?
facts.explanation: Glaucoma derives from Greek 'glaukos' meaning grey-blue, gleaming, or cloudy — describing the bluish-grey appearance of the eye in advanced cases. It is a group of eye conditions characterized by damage to the optic nerve, usually (but not always) from elevated intraocular pressure (IOP). Open-angle glaucoma (the most common form) develops slowly and painlessly, causing progressive peripheral vision loss often unnoticed until advanced — earning it the name 'silent thief of sight'. It is the leading cause of irreversible blindness worldwide.
facts.statement: Glaucoma derives from Greek 'glaukos' (grey-blue/cloudy), describing elevated intraocular pressure that progressively damages the optic nerve.
facts.wowFactor: Ancient Egyptians described a condition called 'water on the eye' that blinded the elderly — likely glaucoma. For 4,000 years there was no treatment. Today, eye drops that reduce pressure, taken daily, can prevent blindness — a straightforward intervention if caught early.

variants.question: The Greek root 'glaukos' in glaucoma originally described what color or appearance?
Why is open-angle glaucoma called the 'silent thief of sight'?
variants.correctAnswer: It causes painless, gradual peripheral vision loss often unnoticed until advanced
distractors: It causes sudden total blindness overnight
It only affects one eye at a time
It causes pain but no visible changes

facts.visualDescription: Eye cross-section with elevated pressure indicated by arrows, damaged optic nerve highlighted, word GLAUCOMA with Greek root GLAUKOS labeled

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from ot/o (ear) + -sclerosis (hardening), meaning abnormal bone growth in the middle ear causing progressive conductive hearing loss?
facts.explanation: Otosclerosis breaks down as: ot/o (ear, from Greek 'ous/otos') + -sclerosis (hardening, from Greek 'skleros'). Abnormal bone remodeling occurs in the bony labyrinth of the inner ear, typically causing the stapes (the smallest bone in the body) to become fixed to the oval window, preventing sound transmission. It typically presents in young adults as progressive conductive hearing loss, often with paracusis Willisii (paradoxical ability to hear better in noisy environments). Surgical stapedectomy — replacing the stapes with a prosthesis — is highly effective.
facts.statement: Otosclerosis means ear hardening, from ot/o (ear) + -sclerosis (hardening), describing abnormal bony fixation of the stapes causing conductive deafness.
facts.wowFactor: Ludwig van Beethoven almost certainly had otosclerosis. His hearing loss began in his late 20s (the classic age of onset), was bilateral, and progressive — and he notably kept his piano on the floor to feel vibrations through the floorboards as he went deaf, a classic strategy for otosclerosis patients.

variants.question: Break down 'otosclerosis' — what does ot/o mean?
ot/o (ear) + -sclerosis (hardening) = ___

facts.visualDescription: Middle ear diagram with the stapes highlighted as fused/fixed to oval window, word OTOSCLEROSIS split: OTO + SCLEROSIS

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from pneum/o (lung) + coni/o (dust) + -osis (condition), meaning lung disease caused by inhaling mineral or organic dust particles?
facts.explanation: Pneumoconiosis breaks down as: pneum/o (lung, from Greek 'pneumon') + coni/o (dust, from Greek 'konis') + -osis (condition/disease). It is a group of interstitial lung diseases caused by long-term inhalation of dust particles that trigger fibrosis. Major subtypes include: silicosis (silica dust — miners, sandblasters), asbestosis (asbestos — shipyard workers), coal workers' pneumoconiosis ('black lung' — coal miners), and byssinosis ('brown lung' — textile workers). All cause progressive, irreversible lung scarring.
facts.statement: Pneumoconiosis means dust lung disease, from pneum/o (lung) + coni/o (dust) + -osis (condition), describing fibrosis from inhaled mineral particles.
facts.wowFactor: Coal workers' pneumoconiosis ('black lung') was declared virtually eliminated in the US by the 1990s — then staged a massive comeback. Cases have surged since 2000, driven by new mining techniques exposing workers to higher-silica rock mixed with coal, with miners developing disease at younger ages than ever recorded.

variants.question: Break down 'pneumoconiosis' — what does coni/o mean?
pneum/o (lung) + coni/o (dust) + -osis = ___

facts.visualDescription: Lung cross-section with dark dust particles embedded in scarred tissue, word PNEUMOCONIOSIS split: PNEUMO + CONI + OSIS

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from hepat/o (liver) + -megaly (enlargement), meaning abnormal enlargement of the liver?
facts.explanation: Hepatomegaly breaks down as: hepat/o (liver, from Greek 'hepar/hepatos') + -megaly (enlargement, from Greek 'megas/megale' — great). It describes a liver enlarged beyond normal size (>15 cm by percussion). Hepatomegaly is a physical finding, not a diagnosis in itself — it indicates an underlying cause such as hepatitis, fatty liver disease, congestive heart failure (congestion backs up into the liver), cirrhosis, or malignancy (primary liver cancer or metastases). Clinically detected by palpation or percussion below the right costal margin.
facts.statement: Hepatomegaly means liver enlargement, from hepat/o (liver) + -megaly (enlargement).
facts.wowFactor: The liver is the only internal organ that can regenerate itself. A donor can give 60% of their liver to a recipient — both donor and recipient livers regrow to full functional size within 6-8 weeks. Ancient Greeks may have intuited this: Prometheus's liver was eaten by an eagle each day and regrew overnight.

variants.question: Break down 'hepatomegaly' — what does -megaly mean?
hepat/o (liver) + -megaly (enlargement) = ___

facts.visualDescription: Torso outline with an oversized liver highlighted, word HEPATOMEGALY split: HEPATO + MEGALY

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from splen/o (spleen) + -megaly (enlargement), meaning abnormal enlargement of the spleen?
facts.explanation: Splenomegaly breaks down as: splen/o (spleen, from Greek 'splen') + -megaly (enlargement, from Greek 'megas'). The spleen is normally about the size of a fist and not palpable; splenomegaly occurs when it becomes palpable below the left costal margin. Causes include infections (mononucleosis — 'mono' — is a classic cause, as are malaria and bacterial endocarditis), hematological disorders (leukemia, lymphoma), portal hypertension from liver disease, and autoimmune conditions. An enlarged spleen risks rupture from even mild trauma.
facts.statement: Splenomegaly means spleen enlargement, from splen/o (spleen) + -megaly (enlargement).
facts.wowFactor: Mononucleosis ('mono', caused by Epstein-Barr virus) causes splenomegaly so severe that contact sports are strictly forbidden for 3-4 weeks — a splenic rupture from a tackle can be fatal. Athletes testing positive for mono are benched until ultrasound confirms the spleen has returned to normal size.

variants.question: Break down 'splenomegaly' — what does splen/o mean?
splen/o (spleen) + -megaly (enlargement) = ___

facts.visualDescription: Left upper abdomen with an oversized spleen visible, word SPLENOMEGALY split: SPLENO + MEGALY

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from cardi/o (heart) + -megaly (enlargement), meaning abnormal enlargement of the heart?
facts.explanation: Cardiomegaly breaks down as: cardi/o (heart, from Greek 'kardia') + -megaly (enlargement, from Greek 'megas'). It is defined as a cardiothoracic ratio >0.5 on chest X-ray (heart width more than half the chest width). Cardiomegaly is a sign, not a diagnosis — causes include dilated cardiomyopathy (the heart muscle weakens and the chambers dilate), hypertension (chronic pressure overload), valve disease, and pericardial effusion (fluid around the heart mimicking enlargement). Athletes can develop physiological cardiomegaly ('athlete's heart') from training.
facts.statement: Cardiomegaly means heart enlargement, from cardi/o (heart) + -megaly (enlargement).
facts.wowFactor: Tour de France cyclists develop such pronounced 'athlete's heart' (physiological cardiomegaly) that their resting heart rate can drop to 28-35 bpm — rates that would indicate severe bradycardia and possible cardiac arrest in a non-athlete.

variants.question: Break down 'cardiomegaly' — what does cardi/o mean?
cardi/o (heart) + -megaly (enlargement) = ___

facts.visualDescription: Chest X-ray style illustration with an oversized heart shadow, word CARDIOMEGALY split: CARDIO + MEGALY

facts.quizQuestion: What condition is named from acr/o (extremity/tip) + -megaly (enlargement), meaning enlargement of the hands, feet, and facial features due to excess growth hormone in adults?
facts.explanation: Acromegaly breaks down as: acr/o (extremity, tip, from Greek 'akron' — outermost point) + -megaly (enlargement). In adults with fused growth plates, excess growth hormone (from a pituitary adenoma in 95%+ of cases) cannot cause height increase — instead it causes acral (extremity) growth: enlarged hands, feet, jaw (prognathism), brow, and tongue. Symptoms develop slowly over years and include ring and shoe size increases, coarsened facial features, and headaches from the tumor. Compare gigantism (same excess hormone in children before growth plate fusion — causes height increase).
facts.statement: Acromegaly means extremity enlargement, from acr/o (extremity/tip) + -megaly (enlargement), describing adult growth hormone excess causing enlarged hands, feet, and face.
facts.wowFactor: Andre the Giant, the wrestling icon and actor, had acromegaly from a pituitary tumor. He stood 7'4" and wore size 24 shoes. His condition was never surgically treated, and he died of heart failure at 46 — cardiomegaly from growth hormone excess eventually kills most untreated acromegaly patients.

variants.question: Break down 'acromegaly' — what does acr/o mean?
acr/o (extremity) + -megaly (enlargement) = ___

facts.visualDescription: Hands and feet comparison — normal vs. enlarged, with enlarged jaw and brow, word ACROMEGALY split: ACRO + MEGALY

facts.quizQuestion: What condition derives from the Greek 'oidema' (swelling), describing abnormal accumulation of fluid in body tissues causing visible swelling?
facts.explanation: Edema derives from Greek 'oidema' meaning swelling (from 'oidein' — to swell). It describes the abnormal accumulation of interstitial fluid (fluid outside of blood vessels and cells) in body tissues. Pitting edema — when pressure on the swollen area leaves an indentation (pit) — indicates fluid in the interstitium. Causes are classified by mechanism: increased hydrostatic pressure (heart failure), decreased oncotic pressure (low albumin in liver disease), increased capillary permeability (inflammation, allergy), and impaired lymphatic drainage (lymphedema). Pulmonary edema (fluid in the lungs) is a medical emergency.
facts.statement: Edema means swelling (Greek 'oidema'), describing abnormal interstitial fluid accumulation in body tissues.
facts.wowFactor: Astronauts develop facial edema (and a characteristic 'puffy face') within hours of reaching microgravity — without gravity pulling fluid downward, it redistributes cephalad (toward the head). Meanwhile their legs become thin as fluid leaves them. Their sinuses fill and they get what astronauts call 'stuffy head syndrome'.

variants.question: The Greek root 'oidema' in edema means:
What is 'pitting edema'?
variants.correctAnswer: Edema where pressing the swollen area leaves an indentation (pit)
distractors: Edema caused by pit viper venom
Edema with small ulcerations on the skin

facts.visualDescription: Ankle and lower leg with visible swelling, finger pressing in to show pitting, word EDEMA with Greek root OIDEMA labeled

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical removal of the appendix called?
facts.explanation: Appendectomy: appendic/o (appendix) + -ectomy (surgical removal). One of the most common emergency surgeries, performed for appendicitis before rupture.
facts.statement: Appendectomy is the surgical removal of the appendix.
facts.wowFactor: The suffix -ectomy always means 'surgical removal' — from the Greek ektome (excision). Once you know -ectomy, you can decode dozens of surgical terms instantly!

variants.question: What does 'appendectomy' mean, breaking down its word parts?
A patient with appendicitis needs emergency surgery to remove the appendix — this procedure is called a(n) ___?

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical removal of the gallbladder called?
facts.explanation: Cholecystectomy: chol/e (bile) + cyst/o (bladder/sac) + -ectomy (surgical removal). The gallbladder stores bile produced by the liver. This is the most common elective abdominal surgery.
facts.statement: Cholecystectomy is the surgical removal of the gallbladder.
facts.wowFactor: Chole- means bile and cysto- means bladder — so cholecystectomy literally means 'bile-bladder removal'. The same roots appear in cholesterol and cystoscopy!

variants.question: Break down 'cholecystectomy' — what organ is removed?
Gallstones blocking bile flow require removal of the gallbladder — this surgery is called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word CHOLECYSTECTOMY split into CHOL/E + CYST/O + ECTOMY with a gallbladder diagram

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical removal of a breast called?
facts.explanation: Mastectomy: mast/o (breast) + -ectomy (surgical removal). Performed primarily for breast cancer treatment. A lumpectomy removes only the tumor, while a mastectomy removes the whole breast.
facts.statement: Mastectomy is the surgical removal of a breast.
facts.wowFactor: Mast/o comes from the Greek mastos (breast) — the same root appears in mastitis (breast inflammation) and the Amazon warrior women whose name some etymologists link to this root!

variants.question: What does 'mast/o' mean in medical terminology?
A surgeon removing an entire breast for cancer treatment performs a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word MASTECTOMY split into MAST/O + ECTOMY with a medical cross symbol

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical removal of the uterus called?
facts.explanation: Hysterectomy: hyster/o (uterus) + -ectomy (surgical removal). The hyster- root comes from Greek hystera (uterus). It is one of the most commonly performed gynecological surgeries.
facts.statement: Hysterectomy is the surgical removal of the uterus.
facts.wowFactor: Hystero- comes from the Greek for 'womb' — ancient Greeks believed the uterus caused erratic emotional behavior, giving us the word 'hysteria'! Medical etymology can be surprisingly revealing about historical attitudes.

variants.question: What does 'hyster/o' mean in medical terminology?
Surgical removal of the uterus to treat fibroids or cancer is called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word HYSTERECTOMY split into HYSTER/O + ECTOMY with uterus anatomical diagram

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical removal of the thyroid gland called?
facts.explanation: Thyroidectomy: thyroid (thyroid gland) + -ectomy (surgical removal). The thyroid gland in the neck produces hormones regulating metabolism. Performed for thyroid cancer, goiter, or hyperthyroidism.
facts.statement: Thyroidectomy is the surgical removal of the thyroid gland.
facts.wowFactor: The thyroid is named for its shield shape — thyreoeides in Greek means 'shield-shaped'. So thyroidectomy literally means 'removal of the shield-shaped gland'!

variants.question: Where is the thyroid gland located, making thyroidectomy a neck surgery?
Removal of the thyroid gland to treat thyroid cancer is called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word THYROIDECTOMY split into THYROID + ECTOMY with butterfly-shaped thyroid gland diagram

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical removal of the tonsils called?
facts.explanation: Tonsillectomy: tonsill/o (tonsil) + -ectomy (surgical removal). The tonsils are lymphoid tissue at the back of the throat. This is one of the most common pediatric surgeries, performed for recurrent tonsillitis or obstructive sleep apnea.
facts.statement: Tonsillectomy is the surgical removal of the tonsils.
facts.wowFactor: Tonsillectomy is so common it's often a child's first surgery — over half a million are performed in the US each year! The tonsil name comes from Latin tonsillae, akin to the word for 'mooring posts' due to their paired shape.

variants.question: What does 'tonsill/o' refer to in medical terminology?
variants.correctAnswer: Tonsils (lymphoid tissue at back of throat)

variants.question: Frequent strep throat may lead a doctor to recommend removal of the tonsils — this operation is called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word TONSILLECTOMY split into TONSILL/O + ECTOMY with throat diagram highlighting tonsil locations

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical removal of a kidney called?
facts.explanation: Nephrectomy: nephr/o (kidney) + -ectomy (surgical removal). The nephro- root comes from Greek nephros (kidney). Performed for kidney cancer, severe kidney disease, or living-donor transplant.
facts.statement: Nephrectomy is the surgical removal of a kidney.
facts.wowFactor: Nephr/o (kidney) appears in nephrology, nephritis, and nephron — the basic functional unit of the kidney. One healthy kidney is sufficient for normal life, which is why living donors can give one kidney!

variants.question: What does 'nephr/o' mean in medical terminology?
A surgeon removes a diseased kidney in a procedure called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word NEPHRECTOMY split into NEPHR/O + ECTOMY with kidney anatomical diagram

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical removal of the spleen called?
facts.explanation: Splenectomy: splen/o (spleen) + -ectomy (surgical removal). The spleen filters blood and supports immune function. Splenectomy is performed after traumatic rupture or for hematologic conditions like immune thrombocytopenia.
facts.statement: Splenectomy is the surgical removal of the spleen.
facts.wowFactor: After splenectomy, patients need vaccinations against encapsulated bacteria (pneumococcus, meningococcus, Haemophilus) because the spleen is uniquely important for clearing these organisms from the blood!

variants.question: What does 'splen/o' mean in medical terminology?
Emergency surgery after a ruptured spleen involves removal of the organ in a procedure called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word SPLENECTOMY split into SPLEN/O + ECTOMY with spleen diagram showing its left-side abdominal position

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical removal of all or part of the stomach called?
facts.explanation: Gastrectomy: gastr/o (stomach) + -ectomy (surgical removal). The gastr- root comes from Greek gaster (stomach). Performed for stomach cancer or severe peptic ulcers. Compare gastroscopy (visual examination of the stomach).
facts.statement: Gastrectomy is the surgical removal of all or part of the stomach.
facts.wowFactor: Gastr/o appears everywhere in medicine: gastritis (stomach inflammation), gastroenterology, gastroscopy — and even 'gastronomy', the art of good eating! Same Greek root gaster meaning belly.

variants.question: Compare gastrectomy vs gastroscopy — what does the -ectomy vs -scopy difference indicate?
Stomach cancer may require partial or total removal of the stomach — this procedure is called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word GASTRECTOMY split into GASTR/O + ECTOMY with stomach diagram

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical removal of an entire lung called?
facts.explanation: Pneumonectomy: pneumon/o (lung) + -ectomy (surgical removal). The pneumon- root comes from Greek pneumon (lung). This is the most radical lung resection, typically performed for lung cancer involving a central tumor. A lobectomy removes just one lobe.
facts.statement: Pneumonectomy is the surgical removal of an entire lung.
facts.wowFactor: After pneumonectomy, the remaining lung compensates by expanding slightly and the mediastinum shifts toward the empty side. People can live with one lung — it just limits strenuous activity!

variants.question: What does 'pneumon/o' mean in medical terminology?
The most extensive lung surgery — removing an entire lung — is called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word PNEUMONECTOMY split into PNEUMON/O + ECTOMY with lung pair diagram, one highlighted for removal

facts.quizQuestion: What is a surgical incision into the trachea (windpipe) to create a temporary airway called?
facts.explanation: Tracheotomy: trache/o (trachea/windpipe) + -otomy (surgical incision). Creates a temporary opening for emergency airway access. Contrast with tracheostomy (-ostomy = permanent opening). The -otomy suffix always means incision, not removal.
facts.statement: Tracheotomy is a surgical incision into the trachea to establish an emergency airway.
facts.wowFactor: Tracheotomy vs tracheostomy trips up even medical students! Tracheotomy (-otomy) is a temporary incision; tracheostomy (-ostomy) creates a permanent stoma. Same organ, two different operations!

variants.question: What does the suffix -otomy mean in medical terminology?
An ER surgeon cuts into the trachea for emergency airway access — this temporary incision is called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word TRACHEOTOMY split into TRACHE/O + OTOMY with trachea diagram and incision arrow

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical procedure that involves cutting into the skull to access the brain called?
facts.explanation: Craniotomy: crani/o (skull) + -otomy (surgical incision). A section of skull is temporarily removed to access the brain, then replaced. One of the most dramatic surgical procedures in neurosurgery.
facts.statement: Craniotomy is a surgical incision into the skull to access the brain.
facts.wowFactor: Trepanation — drilling holes in skulls — is one of humanity's oldest surgeries, with evidence from 8000 BCE! Modern craniotomy is its descendant, now guided by MRI with millimeter precision.

variants.question: What does 'crani/o' mean in medical terminology?
A neurosurgeon opens the skull to remove a brain tumor in a procedure called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word CRANIOTOMY split into CRANI/O + OTOMY with skull diagram showing bone flap removal

facts.quizQuestion: What is a surgical incision through the chest wall to access the thoracic cavity called?
facts.explanation: Thoracotomy: thorac/o (chest/thorax) + -otomy (surgical incision). Provides access to the lungs, heart, esophagus, and great vessels. A posterolateral thoracotomy is the classic approach for lung surgery.
facts.statement: Thoracotomy is a surgical incision through the chest wall to access the thoracic cavity.
facts.wowFactor: A resuscitative thoracotomy in the ER — cracking open the chest to manually pump the heart — can save lives when cardiac arrest results from trauma. It's as dramatic as it sounds!

variants.question: What does 'thorac/o' mean in medical terminology?
A surgeon cuts through the chest wall to reach the lungs in a procedure called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word THORACOTOMY split into THORAC/O + OTOMY with chest cross-section diagram

facts.quizQuestion: What is a large surgical incision through the abdominal wall to access the abdominal cavity called?
facts.explanation: Laparotomy: lapar/o (abdominal wall/flank) + -otomy (surgical incision). The lapar- root comes from Greek lapara (flank). An exploratory laparotomy is performed when the cause of abdominal pathology is unknown. Compare laparoscopy (minimally invasive visualization).
facts.statement: Laparotomy is a surgical incision through the abdominal wall to access the abdominal cavity.
facts.wowFactor: Before laparoscopy, every abdominal surgery required a laparotomy — a large open incision. Laparoscopy (camera through tiny holes) revolutionized surgery in the 1980s, making 'open' laparotomy far less common!

variants.question: Laparotomy vs laparoscopy — both access the abdomen, but how do they differ?
variants.correctAnswer: Laparotomy = large open incision; laparoscopy = minimally invasive camera
distractors: Laparotomy = camera visualization; laparoscopy = large incision
Laparotomy = bowel surgery; laparoscopy = liver surgery
Laparotomy = emergency only; laparoscopy = elective only

variants.question: A surgeon makes a large abdominal incision to explore unexplained internal bleeding — this open procedure is called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word LAPAROTOMY split into LAPAR/O + OTOMY with abdomen diagram showing incision line

facts.quizQuestion: What is the medical term for making an incision into a vein to draw blood or relieve pressure?
facts.explanation: Phlebotomy: phleb/o (vein) + -otomy (surgical incision). From Greek phleps (vein) + tome (cutting). Today used broadly for any blood draw. Historically, bloodletting (therapeutic phlebotomy) was used for conditions like hemochromatosis — still a valid treatment today!
facts.statement: Phlebotomy is an incision into a vein, used today to mean drawing blood for testing or donation.
facts.wowFactor: Phlebotomy was the most common medical procedure for 2,000 years — ancient physicians drained blood to 'rebalance humors'. Today it's still the most performed invasive procedure, just for drawing blood samples!

variants.question: What does 'phleb/o' mean in medical terminology?
A lab technician draws blood by puncturing a vein — this procedure is broadly called ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word PHLEBOTOMY split into PHLEB/O + OTOMY with vein cross-section and needle diagram

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical creation of an opening from the colon through the abdominal wall called?
facts.explanation: Colostomy: col/o (colon) + -ostomy (surgical creation of an opening/stoma). The stoma (Greek for 'mouth') diverts fecal matter into an external bag. Performed after colon cancer resection, Crohn's disease, or traumatic injury. Compare colotomy (incision into colon, -otomy).
facts.statement: Colostomy is the surgical creation of an opening from the colon to the abdominal surface.
facts.wowFactor: The suffix -ostomy (from Greek stoma, mouth) creates a new 'mouth' — an artificial opening. Colostomy, ileostomy, gastrostomy all create external openings, while -otomy just cuts in temporarily!

variants.question: What does the suffix -ostomy mean in medical terminology?
variants.correctAnswer: Surgical creation of a permanent opening (stoma)

variants.question: After colon cancer surgery, waste is diverted through an abdominal opening in a procedure called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word COLOSTOMY split into COL/O + OSTOMY with colon diagram and stoma illustration

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical creation of a permanent opening in the trachea through the neck called?
facts.explanation: Tracheostomy: trache/o (trachea) + -ostomy (creation of a permanent opening). Creates a stoma (permanent hole) in the neck for long-term ventilation. Key distinction: tracheotomy (-otomy) is a temporary incision; tracheostomy (-ostomy) is a permanent stoma.
facts.statement: Tracheostomy is the surgical creation of a permanent opening in the trachea through the neck.
facts.wowFactor: The tracheotomy vs tracheostomy distinction is medically critical: -otomy is temporary (stitched closed later), -ostomy is permanent. Same trachea, opposite long-term outcomes — suffix mastery saves lives in communication!

variants.question: How does tracheostomy differ from tracheotomy?
variants.correctAnswer: Tracheostomy creates a permanent stoma; tracheotomy is a temporary incision
distractors: Tracheostomy is temporary; tracheotomy is permanent
They are synonyms for the same procedure
Tracheostomy uses a tube; tracheotomy uses a camera

variants.question: A long-term ventilator-dependent patient needs a permanent neck airway opening called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word TRACHEOSTOMY split into TRACHE/O + OSTOMY with trachea diagram showing permanent stoma tube

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical creation of an opening from the ileum (small intestine) through the abdominal wall called?
facts.explanation: Ileostomy: ile/o (ileum, the final section of small intestine) + -ostomy (creation of a permanent opening). Liquid waste exits through the stoma since the colon is bypassed. Common in Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis when the colon must be removed.
facts.statement: Ileostomy is the surgical creation of an opening from the ileum to the abdominal surface.
facts.wowFactor: The small intestine has three sections: duodenum, jejunum, and ileum. An ileostomy uses the ileum (last section) — its output is liquid because the colon (which absorbs water) is bypassed entirely!

variants.question: What does 'ile/o' refer to in medical terminology?

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical creation of an opening through the abdominal wall directly into the stomach for tube feeding called?
facts.explanation: Gastrostomy: gastr/o (stomach) + -ostomy (creation of a permanent opening). A feeding tube is inserted directly into the stomach. Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) is the minimally invasive version. Used when patients cannot swallow safely.
facts.statement: Gastrostomy is the surgical creation of an opening into the stomach through the abdominal wall for tube feeding.
facts.wowFactor: The PEG tube (Percutaneous Endoscopic Gastrostomy) uses a camera down the throat while a needle comes in from outside — the light from the scope guides exactly where to place the tube. Precision from two directions at once!

variants.question: What is the difference between gastrectomy and gastrostomy?
variants.correctAnswer: Gastrectomy removes the stomach; gastrostomy creates an opening into it
distractors: Gastrectomy creates an opening; gastrostomy removes the stomach
Both remove the stomach but via different approaches
Gastrectomy is for cancer; gastrostomy is for ulcers

variants.question: A patient who cannot swallow receives a direct stomach feeding tube via a procedure called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word GASTROSTOMY split into GASTR/O + OSTOMY with stomach diagram showing tube placement through abdominal wall

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical creation of an opening from the kidney directly through the back for urine drainage called?
facts.explanation: Nephrostomy: nephr/o (kidney) + -ostomy (creation of a permanent opening). A tube is inserted percutaneously (through the skin) into the renal pelvis to drain urine when the normal urinary pathway is blocked. Compare nephrectomy (removal) and nephrotomy (incision).
facts.statement: Nephrostomy is the creation of an opening into the kidney for direct urine drainage.
facts.wowFactor: A nephrostomy tube bypasses the entire ureter and bladder — urine drains directly from the kidney through the back. The same nephr/o root appears in nephron, nephritis, nephrotic syndrome, and nephrectomy!

variants.question: Compare nephrostomy, nephrotomy, and nephrectomy — what suffix corresponds to each action?
distractors: All three suffixes mean different types of removal

variants.question: A blocked ureter requires direct kidney drainage via a tube through the back — this procedure is called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word NEPHROSTOMY split into NEPHR/O + OSTOMY with kidney diagram showing posterior tube placement

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical repair or reshaping of the nose called?
facts.explanation: Rhinoplasty: rhin/o (nose) + -plasty (surgical repair/reshaping). The -plasty suffix comes from Greek plassein (to mold). Performed for cosmetic reshaping or functional correction (deviated septum, breathing obstruction).
facts.statement: Rhinoplasty is the surgical repair or reshaping of the nose.
facts.wowFactor: Rhinoplasty was performed in ancient India as early as 600 BCE — surgeons used cheek skin flaps to reconstruct noses. The technique was brought to Europe in the 1800s and is the ancestor of modern plastic surgery!

variants.question: What does 'rhin/o' mean in medical terminology?
A surgeon reshapes a patient's nose for cosmetic or breathing purposes in a procedure called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word RHINOPLASTY split into RHIN/O + PLASTY with nose profile diagram

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical reconstruction or replacement of a joint called?
facts.explanation: Arthroplasty: arthr/o (joint) + -plasty (surgical repair/reshaping). The arthr- root comes from Greek arthron (joint). Total hip and knee replacements are the most common arthroplasties performed, restoring mobility in arthritis patients.
facts.statement: Arthroplasty is the surgical reconstruction or replacement of a joint.
facts.wowFactor: Over 1 million knee replacements and 450,000 hip replacements are performed in the US each year — arthroplasty is one of the most successful surgeries in medicine, with 90%+ of patients reporting significant pain relief!

variants.question: What does 'arthr/o' mean in medical terminology?
A worn-out hip joint replaced with a prosthetic implant undergoes a procedure called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word ARTHROPLASTY split into ARTHR/O + PLASTY with hip joint replacement diagram

facts.quizQuestion: What is the procedure to mechanically widen a narrowed or obstructed blood vessel called?
facts.explanation: Angioplasty: angi/o (vessel) + -plasty (surgical repair/reshaping). A balloon catheter is inflated inside the narrowed vessel. Coronary angioplasty (PCI — percutaneous coronary intervention) restores blood flow in heart attacks.
facts.statement: Angioplasty is the procedure to widen narrowed blood vessels, typically using a balloon catheter.
facts.wowFactor: Andreas Grüntzig performed the first balloon angioplasty in a conscious patient in 1977. The patient walked out of the hospital! Today, angioplasty with stenting saves over a million lives a year from heart attacks.

variants.question: What does 'angi/o' mean in medical terminology?
A cardiologist inflates a balloon in a blocked coronary artery to restore blood flow — this is called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word ANGIOPLASTY split into ANGI/O + PLASTY with artery cross-section showing balloon inflation

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical repair or grafting of skin called?
facts.explanation: Dermoplasty: derm/o (skin) + -plasty (surgical repair/reshaping). Also called dermatoplasty (dermat/o = skin). Used for burn reconstruction, wound closure, or scar revision. The derm- root comes from Greek derma (skin).
facts.statement: Dermoplasty is the surgical repair or grafting of skin.
facts.wowFactor: Derm/o and dermat/o both mean skin — that's why you'll see both dermoplasty AND dermatoplasty for the same procedure. Dermatology, epidermis, and hypodermic needle all share the same Greek root derma!

variants.question: What does 'derm/o' mean in medical terminology?
A burn surgeon repairs damaged skin with grafts in a procedure called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word DERMOPLASTY split into DERM/O + PLASTY with skin layer diagram

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical repair of the eyelid(s) — removing excess skin or fat — called?
facts.explanation: Blepharoplasty: blephar/o (eyelid) + -plasty (surgical repair). The blephar- root comes from Greek blepharon (eyelid). Performed for drooping eyelids (ptosis) that impair vision, or cosmetically to remove excess eyelid skin.
facts.statement: Blepharoplasty is the surgical repair of the eyelids, removing excess skin or fat.
facts.wowFactor: Blephar/o (eyelid) appears in blepharitis (eyelid inflammation) and blepharospasm (involuntary eyelid twitching). At 5 syllables, blepharoplasty is one of the most tongue-twisting surgical terms — but it's just eyelid + repair!

variants.question: What does 'blephar/o' mean in medical terminology?
Drooping eyelids impair a patient's vision — surgical correction of the eyelid is called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word BLEPHAROPLASTY split into BLEPHAR/O + PLASTY with eye diagram highlighting eyelid

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical fusion of two bones at a joint to eliminate movement and relieve pain called?
facts.explanation: Arthrodesis: arthr/o (joint) + -desis (binding/fusion). The -desis suffix comes from Greek desis (binding). Unlike arthroplasty (replacement), arthrodesis permanently immobilizes the joint by fusing the bones together — trading mobility for pain relief.
facts.statement: Arthrodesis is the surgical fusion of bones at a joint to eliminate painful movement.
facts.wowFactor: The -desis suffix means 'binding together' — it appears in syndesmosis (ligament binding), tenodesis (tendon anchoring), and pleurodesis (lung lining fusion). Arthrodesis trades motion for stability — permanent but effective for severe arthritis!

variants.question: What does the suffix -desis mean in medical terminology?
Severe ankle arthritis is treated by fusing the joint bones — this procedure is called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word ARTHRODESIS split into ARTHR/O + DESIS with joint fusion diagram showing bone screws

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical repair of a hernia by suturing the weakened tissue called?
facts.explanation: Herniorrhaphy: herni/o (hernia) + -rrhaphy (surgical suturing/repair). The -rrhaphy suffix comes from Greek rhaphe (seam/suture). The weakened abdominal wall is stitched back together. Compare hernioplasty (mesh reinforcement).
facts.statement: Herniorrhaphy is the surgical repair of a hernia using sutures.
facts.wowFactor: The suffix -rrhaphy (suture repair) appears in many surgical terms: herniorrhaphy, enterorrhaphy (intestine repair), colorrhaphy (colon repair). It always means sewing tissue back together — surgical needlework!

variants.question: What does the suffix -rrhaphy mean in medical terminology?
variants.correctAnswer: Surgical suturing or repair by sewing

variants.question: A surgeon stitches a weakened abdominal wall to repair a hernia — this suture repair is called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word HERNIORRHAPHY split into HERNI/O + RRHAPHY with hernia diagram showing suture lines

facts.quizQuestion: What is the procedure that uses shock waves to break up kidney stones or other calculi called?
facts.explanation: Lithotripsy: lith/o (stone) + -tripsy (crushing). The lith- root comes from Greek lithos (stone) and -tripsy from tribein (to crush). Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) uses external sound waves to shatter stones without incision.
facts.statement: Lithotripsy is the procedure using shock waves to crush kidney stones or other calculi.
facts.wowFactor: Lithotripsy is one of medicine's most elegant solutions — sound waves focused from outside the body shatter stones inside it. No incisions! The suffix -tripsy (crushing) also appears in neurotripsy and phlebotripsy!

variants.question: What does 'lith/o' mean in medical terminology?
A patient with kidney stones is treated with external shock waves that shatter the stones — this is called ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word LITHOTRIPSY split into LITH/O + TRIPSY with kidney diagram showing shock wave paths converging on stone

facts.quizQuestion: What is the procedure of inserting a needle into the abdominal cavity to drain excess fluid (ascites) called?
facts.explanation: Paracentesis: para- (beside/near) + kentesis (puncture). The -centesis suffix always means surgical puncture to withdraw fluid. Specifically refers to abdominal fluid drainage. Compare thoracentesis (chest), pericardiocentesis (heart sac), arthrocentesis (joint).
facts.statement: Paracentesis is needle puncture into the abdominal cavity to drain excess fluid (ascites).
facts.wowFactor: The -centesis suffix (Greek kentesis, puncture) lets you locate where fluid is being drained: para- (abdominal), thoraco- (chest), pericardio- (heart sac), arthro- (joint), amnio- (amniotic sac). One suffix, five different procedures!

variants.question: What does the suffix -centesis mean in medical terminology?
Liver cirrhosis causes fluid buildup in the abdomen — draining this fluid with a needle is called ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word PARACENTESIS split into PARA + CENTESIS with abdomen diagram showing needle insertion site

facts.quizQuestion: What is the surgical fixation or repositioning of an ovary (as before radiation therapy) called?
facts.explanation: Oophoropexy: oophor/o (ovary) + -pexy (surgical fixation). The -pexy suffix means 'surgical fastening/fixation' (from Greek pexis, fixation). The ovaries are moved out of a radiation field to preserve fertility, then may be repositioned afterward.
facts.statement: Oophoropexy is the surgical fixation or repositioning of an ovary, often to preserve fertility before radiation.
facts.wowFactor: The -pexy suffix (surgical fixation) appears in orchiopexy (anchoring undescended testicle), nephropexy (securing a floating kidney), and gastropexy (anchoring stomach). Oophoropexy saves ovarian function from radiation by simply moving the ovaries out of the way!

variants.question: What does the suffix -pexy mean in medical terminology?
Before pelvic radiation, a surgeon moves the ovaries out of the radiation field in a procedure called a(n) ___?

facts.visualDescription: Word OOPHOROPEXY split into OOPHOR/O + PEXY with pelvic diagram showing ovary repositioning

facts.quizQuestion: What diagnostic procedure visually examines the inner lining of the colon using a flexible scope?
facts.explanation: Colonoscopy: col/o (colon) + -scopy (visual examination). A flexible camera-tipped tube is inserted rectally to inspect the large intestine. Commonly used for colorectal cancer screening and polyp removal.
facts.statement: Colonoscopy is the visual examination of the colon using an endoscopic scope.
facts.wowFactor: A colonoscopy scope can be up to 1.5 metres long — long enough to travel the entire length of the large intestine!

variants.question: What does 'colonoscopy' mean when broken into its word parts?

facts.quizQuestion: What broad term describes any procedure using a flexible tube with a camera to visually examine internal organs?
facts.explanation: Endoscopy: endo- (within) + -scopy (visual examination). The umbrella term for inserting a scope inside the body to view internal structures. Colonoscopy, gastroscopy, and bronchoscopy are all types of endoscopy.
facts.statement: Endoscopy is the visual examination of internal organs using a flexible instrument inserted into the body.
facts.wowFactor: The first endoscope was built in 1806 — a metal tube lit by candles! Modern fibre-optic endoscopes emerged in the 1950s.

variants.question: What does 'endoscopy' mean broken into its word parts?
endo- (within) + -scopy (visual examination) = ?

facts.quizQuestion: What procedure uses a small camera inserted into a joint to visually examine the interior?
facts.explanation: Arthroscopy: arthr/o (joint) + -scopy (visual examination). A thin camera called an arthroscope is inserted through a small incision to view joint structures such as cartilage, ligaments, and bone surfaces.
facts.statement: Arthroscopy is the visual examination of the interior of a joint using a small camera.
facts.wowFactor: Arthroscopy revolutionised knee surgery — instead of opening the entire knee, surgeons now work through cuts smaller than a centimetre!

variants.question: What does 'arthroscopy' mean broken into its word parts?
arthr/o (joint) + -scopy (visual examination) = ?

facts.quizQuestion: What procedure visually examines the airways and bronchi of the lungs using a scope?
facts.explanation: Bronchoscopy: bronch/o (bronchus/airway) + -scopy (visual examination). A thin flexible tube is passed through the mouth or nose into the bronchi to visualise the airways, collect samples, and diagnose lung conditions.
facts.statement: Bronchoscopy is the visual examination of the bronchi and airways using a flexible scope.
facts.wowFactor: During a bronchoscopy, doctors can flush the airways with saline and retrieve the fluid — a technique called bronchoalveolar lavage — to diagnose hidden infections.

variants.question: What does 'bronchoscopy' mean broken into its word parts?
bronch/o (bronchus) + -scopy (visual examination) = ?

facts.quizQuestion: What minimally-invasive procedure uses a camera inserted through the abdomen to examine pelvic and abdominal organs?
facts.explanation: Laparoscopy: lapar/o (abdomen/flank) + -scopy (visual examination). A small camera is inserted through a tiny incision in the abdomen, often with CO2 gas inflated to create viewing space. Used diagnostically and surgically.
facts.statement: Laparoscopy is the visual examination of the abdominal cavity using a camera inserted through a small incision.
facts.wowFactor: Laparoscopy is called 'keyhole surgery' — surgeons operate through incisions as small as 5mm, leaving almost no scar compared to open surgery.

facts.quizQuestion: What procedure uses a scope passed through the urethra to visually examine the bladder?
facts.explanation: Cystoscopy: cyst/o (bladder) + -scopy (visual examination). A thin lighted tube is inserted through the urethra into the bladder to diagnose bladder stones, tumours, inflammation, and other urinary tract conditions.
facts.statement: Cystoscopy is the visual examination of the urinary bladder using a scope passed through the urethra.
facts.wowFactor: Cystoscopy can detect bladder cancer at an early, highly treatable stage — making it one of the most important urological diagnostic tools.

variants.question: What does 'cystoscopy' mean broken into its word parts?
cyst/o (bladder) + -scopy (visual examination) = ?

facts.visualDescription: Word CYSTOSCOPY split into CYST/O + SCOPY with a bladder cross-section and scope icon

facts.quizQuestion: What procedure uses a flexible scope passed through the mouth to visually examine the stomach?
facts.explanation: Gastroscopy: gastr/o (stomach) + -scopy (visual examination). A thin flexible endoscope is passed through the mouth, oesophagus, and into the stomach to diagnose ulcers, gastritis, and tumours.
facts.statement: Gastroscopy is the visual examination of the stomach using a flexible endoscope passed through the mouth.
facts.wowFactor: During a gastroscopy, doctors can take biopsies and stop bleeding — all without a single surgical incision!

variants.question: What does 'gastroscopy' mean broken into its word parts?
gastr/o (stomach) + -scopy (visual examination) = ?

facts.visualDescription: Word GASTROSCOPY split into GASTR/O + SCOPY with a stomach outline and flexible scope icon

facts.quizQuestion: What procedure uses a handheld instrument with a light and lenses to visually examine the interior of the eye?
facts.explanation: Ophthalmoscopy: ophthalm/o (eye) + -scopy (visual examination). A doctor shines a bright light into the eye to examine the retina, optic disc, and blood vessels at the back of the eye. Used to detect glaucoma, retinal disease, and diabetes-related eye damage.
facts.statement: Ophthalmoscopy is the visual examination of the interior of the eye using an ophthalmoscope.
facts.wowFactor: The retina is the only place in the body where blood vessels can be directly observed without surgery — making ophthalmoscopy a window into your vascular health!

variants.question: What does 'ophthalmoscopy' mean broken into its word parts?
distractors: Surgical removal of the eye lens

facts.quizQuestion: What imaging technique uses X-rays to produce a recorded image of internal body structures?
facts.explanation: Radiography: radi/o (radiation/X-ray) + -graphy (recording/imaging). X-rays are passed through the body and captured on film or a digital detector. Dense structures like bone absorb more X-rays and appear white; soft tissues appear grey.
facts.statement: Radiography is the technique of recording internal body structures using X-ray radiation.
facts.wowFactor: Wilhelm Röntgen discovered X-rays in 1895 and took the first radiograph of his wife's hand — she saw her own skeleton and declared 'I have seen my death!'

variants.question: What does 'radiography' mean broken into its word parts?

facts.quizQuestion: What X-ray imaging procedure is used specifically to examine breast tissue for tumours and abnormalities?
facts.explanation: Mammography: mamm/o (breast) + -graphy (recording/imaging). Low-dose X-rays are used to image breast tissue. The breast is compressed between two plates to spread the tissue and produce clearer images. Used as a primary screening tool for breast cancer.
facts.statement: Mammography is the X-ray imaging of breast tissue, used primarily for breast cancer screening.
facts.wowFactor: Regular mammography screening reduces breast cancer mortality by approximately 20% — it remains one of the most effective cancer detection tools available.

variants.question: What does 'mammography' mean broken into its word parts?
mamm/o (breast) + -graphy (recording) = ?

facts.visualDescription: Word MAMMOGRAPHY split into MAMM/O + GRAPHY with a breast X-ray image icon

facts.quizQuestion: What imaging procedure uses contrast dye and X-rays to record images of blood vessels?
facts.explanation: Angiography: angi/o (vessel) + -graphy (recording/imaging). A contrast dye is injected into the bloodstream and X-rays are taken to create images of arteries and veins. Used to detect blockages, aneurysms, and vascular malformations.
facts.statement: Angiography is the X-ray imaging of blood vessels using an injected contrast dye.
facts.wowFactor: Coronary angiography can pinpoint the exact millimetre of an arterial blockage — guiding surgeons to the precise spot for a stent or bypass.

variants.question: What does 'angiography' mean broken into its word parts?
angi/o (vessel) + -graphy (recording) = ?

facts.quizQuestion: What imaging procedure uses sound waves to record moving images of the heart's structure and function?
facts.explanation: Echocardiography: echo (sound/reflection) + cardi/o (heart) + -graphy (recording). Ultrasound waves are bounced off heart structures to produce real-time images of the beating heart, including valves, chambers, and blood flow.
facts.statement: Echocardiography is the ultrasound imaging of the heart, using reflected sound waves to record cardiac structure and function.
facts.wowFactor: Echocardiography lets doctors watch the heart valves opening and closing in real time — a live movie of your heart in action, using only sound waves!

variants.question: What does 'echocardiography' mean broken into its word parts?
variants.correctAnswer: Recording of the heart using reflected sound

facts.quizQuestion: What procedure records the brain's electrical activity using electrodes attached to the scalp?
facts.explanation: Electroencephalography: electr/o (electricity) + encephal/o (brain) + -graphy (recording). Electrodes placed on the scalp detect tiny electrical signals produced by neuron activity. The recording (EEG) is used to diagnose epilepsy, sleep disorders, and brain injury.
facts.statement: Electroencephalography (EEG) is the recording of the brain's electrical activity using scalp electrodes.
facts.wowFactor: The brain generates about 23 watts of electrical power continuously — enough to power a dim light bulb — and EEG captures those signals from outside the skull!

variants.question: What does 'electroencephalography' mean broken into its word parts?
variants.correctAnswer: Recording of electrical activity of the brain
distractors: Recording of electrical activity of the heart

facts.quizQuestion: What procedure records the heart's electrical activity using electrodes placed on the skin?
facts.explanation: Electrocardiography: electr/o (electricity) + cardi/o (heart) + -graphy (recording). Electrodes on the skin detect the heart's electrical impulses as it beats. The resulting ECG trace shows the timing and pattern of each heartbeat, used to diagnose arrhythmias and heart attacks.
facts.statement: Electrocardiography (ECG/EKG) is the recording of the heart's electrical activity using skin electrodes.
facts.wowFactor: An ECG can detect a heart attack that happened days ago — the electrical signature of damaged heart muscle persists in the waveform long after the event.

facts.quizQuestion: What imaging technique records internal body structures using high-frequency sound waves reflected from tissues?
facts.explanation: Ultrasonography: ultra- (beyond/above) + son/o (sound) + -graphy (recording). A transducer emits high-frequency sound waves that bounce off internal structures; the returning echoes are processed into real-time images. No radiation is involved, making it safe for pregnancy monitoring.
facts.statement: Ultrasonography is the recording of internal body images using reflected high-frequency sound waves.
facts.wowFactor: Ultrasound frequencies used in medicine are typically 1–20 MHz — thousands of times higher than the upper limit of human hearing (20 kHz)!

ultra- (beyond) + son/o (sound) + -graphy (recording) = ?

facts.quizQuestion: What is the NAME of the recorded output (the trace or image) produced by measuring the heart's electrical activity?
facts.explanation: Electrocardiogram: electr/o (electricity) + cardi/o (heart) + -gram (record/image). The ECG is the actual printed or digital trace — the record itself — produced by the process of electrocardiography. The P, QRS, and T waves on the trace represent different phases of the heartbeat.
facts.statement: An electrocardiogram (ECG or EKG) is the recorded tracing of the heart's electrical activity.
facts.wowFactor: The distinctive 'PQRST' wave pattern on an ECG was named by Willem Einthoven in 1895 — he chose those letters arbitrarily to leave room for future discoveries before and after!

facts.quizQuestion: What is the term for the X-ray image or record produced from imaging the breast?
facts.explanation: Mammogram: mamm/o (breast) + -gram (record/image). The mammogram is the actual image produced — the X-ray film or digital record of the breast tissue. Distinguishing the -gram (the image) from mammography (the process) is a key medical terminology concept.
facts.statement: A mammogram is the X-ray image of the breast produced during mammography.
facts.wowFactor: A single mammogram exposes each breast to roughly 0.4 millisieverts of radiation — less than the background radiation you absorb from the environment over two months.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the term for the recorded tracing of the brain's electrical activity captured via scalp electrodes?
facts.explanation: Electroencephalogram: electr/o (electricity) + encephal/o (brain) + -gram (record/image). The EEG is the actual recorded trace — the printed or digital output showing brain wave patterns. Different wave types (alpha, beta, delta, theta) indicate different brain states.
facts.statement: An electroencephalogram (EEG) is the recorded tracing of the brain's electrical activity.
facts.wowFactor: EEG can detect the precise moment you fall asleep by tracking the transition from alert alpha waves to slow theta waves — your brain's electrical signature changes within seconds!

facts.quizQuestion: What is the term for the X-ray image of blood vessels produced after injecting contrast dye?
facts.explanation: Angiogram: angi/o (vessel) + -gram (record/image). The angiogram is the recorded image produced during angiography — the actual X-ray film or digital image showing the outline of blood vessels filled with contrast dye. Blockages appear as narrow or absent sections.
facts.statement: An angiogram is the recorded X-ray image of blood vessels produced during angiography.
facts.wowFactor: Coronary angiograms are performed on over 1 million patients per year in the US — they remain the gold standard for diagnosing coronary artery disease.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the term for the recorded X-ray image of the spinal cord and surrounding structures after contrast dye injection?
facts.explanation: Myelogram: myel/o (spinal cord/bone marrow) + -gram (record/image). Contrast dye is injected into the spinal canal (subarachnoid space) and X-rays or CT scans are taken. The myelogram reveals the spinal cord, nerve roots, and any compression from herniated discs or tumours.
facts.statement: A myelogram is the recorded image of the spinal cord produced after injecting contrast dye into the spinal canal.
facts.wowFactor: Before MRI existed, myelograms were the only way to visualise spinal cord compression — doctors used oil-based dyes that had to be manually retrieved after the procedure!

The surgical imaging of the vertebrae

variants.question: myel/o (spinal cord) + -gram (record/image) = ?

facts.visualDescription: Word MYELOGRAM split into MYEL/O + GRAM with a spinal column cross-section and contrast dye icon

facts.quizQuestion: What diagnostic measurement test assesses lung function by measuring the volume and speed of air inhaled and exhaled?
facts.explanation: Spirometry: spir/o (breath/breathing) + -metry (measurement). The patient breathes into a mouthpiece connected to a spirometer, which records the volume and flow rate of air. Key measurements include FVC (forced vital capacity) and FEV1, used to diagnose asthma and COPD.
facts.statement: Spirometry is the measurement of lung function by recording the volume and speed of breathing.
facts.wowFactor: Spirometry can detect COPD years before symptoms appear — catching the disease when lungs still have enough reserve to make lifestyle changes meaningful.

variants.question: What does 'spirometry' mean broken into its word parts?
spir/o (breath) + -metry (measurement) = ?

facts.visualDescription: Word SPIROMETRY split into SPIR/O + METRY with a lung outline and flow-volume curve graph icon

facts.quizQuestion: What diagnostic test measures the range and sensitivity of a person's hearing?
facts.explanation: Audiometry: audi/o (hearing/sound) + -metry (measurement). Tones of varying frequency and volume are played through headphones; the patient indicates which they can hear. Results are plotted on an audiogram to identify hearing loss type and degree.
facts.statement: Audiometry is the measurement of hearing ability across a range of frequencies and sound levels.
facts.wowFactor: A normal human ear can hear frequencies from 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz — audiometry maps exactly which part of that range has been lost and by how many decibels.

variants.question: What does 'audiometry' mean broken into its word parts?
distractors: Visual examination of the ear canal

variants.question: audi/o (hearing) + -metry (measurement) = ?

facts.visualDescription: Word AUDIOMETRY split into AUDI/O + METRY with an ear cross-section and audiogram chart icon

facts.quizQuestion: What non-invasive measurement technique uses light wavelengths to determine the oxygen saturation of blood?
facts.explanation: Oximetry: ox/i (oxygen) + -metry (measurement). A pulse oximeter clips onto a fingertip and shines red and infrared light through the skin. Oxygenated and deoxygenated haemoglobin absorb these wavelengths differently, allowing the device to calculate the percentage of haemoglobin carrying oxygen (SpO2).
facts.statement: Oximetry is the non-invasive measurement of blood oxygen saturation using light absorption.
facts.wowFactor: Pulse oximetry became widespread during COVID-19 because it could silently detect 'happy hypoxia' — patients with dangerously low oxygen levels who felt no breathlessness.

variants.question: What does 'oximetry' mean broken into its word parts?
ox/i (oxygen) + -metry (measurement) = ?

facts.visualDescription: Word OXIMETRY split into OX/I + METRY with a fingertip pulse oximeter and SpO2 readout icon

facts.quizQuestion: What health discipline measures vision and examines the eyes to prescribe corrective lenses?
facts.explanation: Optometry: opt/o (eye/vision) + -metry (measurement). Optometrists measure visual acuity, refraction, and eye health. They prescribe glasses and contact lenses and can identify conditions like glaucoma and cataracts. Note: optometrists are not medical doctors (ophthalmologists are).
facts.statement: Optometry is the measurement and examination of vision and eye health, performed by an optometrist.
facts.wowFactor: The Snellen eye chart was invented in 1862 — optometrists still use essentially the same design over 160 years later, with the big 'E' at the top representing 20/200 vision.

variants.question: What does 'optometry' mean broken into its word parts?
opt/o (eye/vision) + -metry (measurement) = ?

facts.visualDescription: Word OPTOMETRY split into OPT/O + METRY with a Snellen eye chart and refraction lens icon

facts.quizQuestion: What diagnostic measurement involves using a device to determine body temperature?
facts.explanation: Thermometry: therm/o (heat/temperature) + -metry (measurement). Thermometers measure body temperature in degrees Celsius or Fahrenheit. Fever is defined as a core body temperature above 38°C (100.4°F) and is a key diagnostic indicator of infection and inflammation.
facts.statement: Thermometry is the measurement of body temperature using a thermometer.
facts.wowFactor: The clinical mercury thermometer was invented in 1714 and took about 25 minutes to give a reading — modern digital thermometers do it in under 10 seconds!

variants.question: What does 'thermometry' mean broken into its word parts?
therm/o (heat) + -metry (measurement) = ?

facts.visualDescription: Word THERMOMETRY split into THERM/O + METRY with a thermometer showing temperature scale icon

facts.quizQuestion: What obstetric term describes the total number of pregnancies a woman has had, regardless of outcome?
facts.explanation: Gravida comes from Latin 'gravidus' (heavy, burdened, pregnant). It counts every pregnancy including the current one, twins count as one pregnancy.
